title: Airline Pilot

rationales.taskAutomation: Autopilot and autoland already fly most of cruise and a share of approach, but takeoff, taxi, weather diversions, and emergencies remain a licensed human responsibility by regulation.
rationales.toolMaturity: Autopilot/autoland systems are decades-mature and near-universal on commercial aircraft; single-pilot or pilotless commercial passenger flight is not a certified or shipped product.
rationales.adoption: Every major carrier uses mature autopilot systems, but that has not reduced flight-deck crew counts; reduced-crew concepts remain in study, not deployment.
rationales.agenticExposure: The role is licensed, safety-critical, and physically present by regulatory mandate; agentic systems don't reach cockpit command authority today.

tasksGrowing: Takeoff, landing, and taxi in degraded or non-standard conditions
Crew leadership and passenger-safety command authority
salarySource: BLS OES May 2024, SOC 53-2011, p25-p75 rounded, high end reflects major-carrier captain pay

defenseSkills.skill: Crew resource management and safety leadership

insight: Autopilot already handles most of the flying hours in a commercial flight, and that has been true for years without displacing pilots, because certification, insurance, and passenger trust require a licensed human in command. Reduced-crew and single-pilot concepts are in FAA-monitored study, not deployment; a real regulatory shift is possible over a decade-plus horizon but is not the near-term picture.
